title: Carbon Tax Rate Prediction for China
subtitle: XGBoost with K-means similarity weighting and SHAP interpretation
oneLiner: A dual-objective policy model that estimates a carbon-tax range balancing emissions reduction and economic stability.
problem: How can a future carbon-tax policy reduce emissions without creating unacceptable pressure on GDP growth?
approach: I built a dual-response regression pipeline with XGBoost, predicting both GDP growth impact and carbon-intensity reduction. To reduce country-level heterogeneity, I used K-means to find samples structurally closer to China and up-weighted them during training.
results: Under the project constraints (carbon intensity reduction above 4 percent and GDP growth above 2 percent), the recommended range is 30-40 USD per ton CO2, with 35 USD as the center recommendation.
meaning: This work shows how machine learning can complement macro policy analysis by turning high-level climate goals into a testable decision interval.

metrics.label: Center Recommendation
metrics.note: Balanced point between growth and abatement

methods.name: XGBoost
methods.explain: Gradient-boosted decision trees used for nonlinear regression with strong tabular performance.

methods.name: K-means Weighting
methods.explain: Countries close to China in feature space were weighted higher to improve relevance.

methods.name: SHAP
methods.explain: Feature-attribution analysis used to explain marginal and interaction effects.

glossary.explain: A boosted-tree algorithm that fits complex nonlinear relations with regularization.
A game-theoretic attribution method showing how each feature contributes to predictions.
Simultaneously modeling economic and environmental outcomes for policy design.

figures.caption: Dual-effect prediction curves: GDP-side and carbon-intensity-side responses.
SHAP analysis highlights key variables and nonlinear policy response patterns.
Model output summary used to identify the feasible tax corridor.

title: K-means MNIST Digit Recognition
subtitle: Unsupervised digit clustering with initialization strategy comparison
oneLiner: A no-label recognition experiment that tests how far classical clustering can go on handwritten digits.
problem: Can an unsupervised clustering algorithm separate handwritten digits effectively without using labels during training?
approach: I ran K-means on MNIST with K=10 and compared Random initialization, K-means++, and Density-based initialization. Evaluation used Accuracy (post-label alignment), Silhouette Score, and ARI, plus convergence behavior under different iteration budgets.
results: Random and Density initialization reached around 0.59 accuracy, while K-means++ reached about 0.58. Density achieved stronger structure metrics (Silhouette around 0.071 and ARI around 0.412) but required more runtime.
meaning: The project clarifies a practical tradeoff: initialization can improve cluster quality, but better structure does not always mean lower runtime.

metrics.label: Best Accuracy
metrics.note: Random and Density initialization in this setup

methods.name: K-means
methods.explain: Iteratively updates centroids to minimize within-cluster distance.

methods.name: Initialization Study
methods.explain: Random, K-means++, and Density seeds were compared for quality and speed.

methods.name: Post-hoc Label Mapping
methods.explain: Cluster ids were mapped to digit labels for fair accuracy estimation.

glossary.explain: Measures cohesion and separation of clusters; higher usually indicates cleaner grouping.
Adjusted Rand Index; compares predicted clusters to ground-truth labels while correcting chance.
How starting centroids are chosen; often critical for final K-means performance.

figures.caption: Comparative experiment outputs across initialization strategies.
Metric table and runtime comparison for Random, K-means++, and Density settings.
Visual evidence of clustering behavior and error modes on handwritten digits.

title: Ensemble Learning on Amazon Reviews
subtitle: TF-IDF text representation with SVM/Tree and Bagging/AdaBoost combinations
oneLiner: A structured comparison of ensemble strategies for text-based rating prediction under sparse high-dimensional features.
problem: How should we combine classical text models and ensemble methods to improve rating prediction from Amazon review text?
approach: I converted reviews into TF-IDF vectors and evaluated four combinations: SVM + Bagging, Tree + Bagging, SVM + AdaBoost, and Tree + AdaBoost. The analysis focused on error behavior (MAE/RMSE), stability, and computational tradeoffs.
results: The experiment confirmed expected bias-variance behavior: Bagging helps unstable learners like trees, while boosting can focus on difficult samples but is more sensitive to noise and compute cost when paired with strong base models.
meaning: For readers beyond ML, this project demonstrates that model performance is not just about one algorithm choice, but also about how data representation and ensemble strategy interact.

methods.name: TF-IDF
methods.explain: Converts text into weighted term vectors emphasizing informative words.

methods.name: Bagging
methods.explain: Reduces variance via bootstrap resampling and model aggregation.

methods.name: AdaBoost
methods.explain: Iteratively reweights hard samples to reduce bias on difficult cases.

glossary.explain: Bootstrap Aggregating; improves stability by averaging many resampled learners.
Adaptive boosting that emphasizes misclassified examples over rounds.
Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency; highlights words that are important in one document but not common everywhere.

figures.caption: Comparison chart of ensemble configurations on review prediction.
Error and performance tradeoff illustration across model combinations.
Model behavior snapshot under shared TF-IDF feature conditions.
